threats.title: LRT Claim Sweeps Entire Contract ETH Balance Instead of Swap Delta
threats.hypothesis_description: In _claim for non-ETH tokens, claimedAmount is set to address(this).balance (absolute) instead of the delta from the swap. Any residual ETH in the contract from any source is swept by the claimer and converted to lpETH. The _fillQuote function correctly computes the delta (boughtETHAmount) but this value is discarded by _claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect where the contract comment acknowledges 'At this point there should not be any ETH in the contract' but enforces no invariant. Any ETH arriving via receive(), selfdestruct, or other mechanisms is silently stolen by the next LRT claimer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimedAmount = address(this).balance used after _fillQuote instead of tracking pre/post delta; _fillQuote computes boughtETHAmount delta internally but only emits it

preconditions: startClaimDate has passed (convertAllETH was called)
User has non-zero LRT token balance
Residual ETH exists in contract (via receive(), selfdestruct, or other source)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: claimedAmount = address(this).balance captures ALL ETH, not just swap output
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any ETH in contract from any source is captured by next LRT claimer
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: profit = residualETH captured as bonus lpETH beyond actual swap value

economic_questions: How much residual ETH can realistically accumulate post-conversion?
Can selfdestruct be used to force ETH into the contract for extraction?
What is the expected contract ETH balance at various lifecycle stages?
validation_requirements: Test with forced ETH via selfdestruct before LRT claim
Verify that _fillQuote boughtETHAmount is truly discarded
Confirm lpETH.deposit accepts the full balance amount

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal LRT balance + ETH for selfdestruct (if forcing ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for claim tx + selfdestruct contract deployment
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to all residual ETH in contract
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially infinite ROI on gas costs if residual ETH exists
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction after residual ETH accumulates

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None beyond LRT balance and valid 0x swap data
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after ETH accumulates in contract post-conversion

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Accidental ETH senders, or protocol if ETH is forced via selfdestruct

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare absolute balance to delta calculation

threats.title: UniswapV3 Calldata Path Validation Bypass via Non-Standard ABI Encoding
threats.hypothesis_description: _decodeUniswapV3Data reads encodedPath data from hardcoded calldata offsets (assuming standard ABI encoding with bytes offset = 0x80). An attacker can craft non-standard but valid ABI encoding where the offset pointer at position 4 points to a different location. The validator reads fake path data at the hardcoded position while the 0x exchange proxy follows the real offset to execute a different path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The assembly decoding skips reading the actual offset pointer at position 4, instead hardcoding offset 132 for encodedPathLength and 164 for path start. This allows inputToken and outputToken validation to be completely bypassed, enabling arbitrary swap routing through attacker-controlled pools.

proof_of_signal.pattern: encodedPathLength read from hardcoded offset (p+96) and inputToken/outputToken from (p+128) / (p+encodedPathLength+108) without reading actual bytes offset from position 4

preconditions: Attacker has LRT token balance to claim
startClaimDate has passed
0x exchange proxy accepts non-standard ABI-encoded calldata

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Hardcoded offsets: encodedPathLength at p+96, inputToken at p+128 vs dynamic ABI offset at position 4
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Fake path at standard position passes validation; real path at custom offset executes on 0x proxy
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker routes through manipulated pools to extract value, or bypasses token/output checks entirely

economic_questions: Does the 0x proxy correctly read non-standard ABI offsets?
Can the real path route through attacker-controlled pools?
Could this bypass allow swapping to non-ETH output tokens?
validation_requirements: Craft non-standard ABI-encoded calldata that passes _validateData but executes different path
Test against 0x exchange proxy to confirm real path execution
Verify if residual approvals from other tokens can be consumed

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard claim gas + pool deployment costs
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on routing manipulation and residual approvals

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker deploys own Uniswap V3 pool for routing
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Contained to attacker's own swap unless combined with approval theft

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol (if combined with residual approval theft), other token depositors
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Residual approved tokens from other claims

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: ABI offset at position 4 vs hardcoded offset 0x80 (128)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare validator-decoded path vs proxy-decoded path

threats.title: TransformERC20 Transformations Array Unvalidated Enables Arbitrary 0x Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: The _validateData function for TransformERC20 only validates inputToken, outputToken, inputTokenAmount, and selector. The transformations array (the 5th parameter of transformERC20) is completely unvalidated. An attacker can include arbitrary transformation steps that execute sub-operations through the 0x proxy, potentially redirecting approved tokens or extracting value through registered transformer contracts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The 0x TransformERC20 feature allows a pipeline of transformations (FillQuoteTransformer, PayTakerTransformer, WethTransformer, etc.). An attacker controls the entire transformations array, which could include operations that interact with other approved tokens, redirect intermediate values, or execute complex multi-step operations that bypass the simple input/output validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _decodeTransformERC20Data only reads first 3 slots (inputToken, outputToken, inputTokenAmount) + selector; transformations array at offset 100+ is never parsed or validated

preconditions: Attacker has LRT token balance to claim
startClaimDate has passed
0x exchange proxy has registered transformers that can be weaponized

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: transformations[] array is forwarded entirely to exchangeProxy without any validation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Arbitrary transformer calls via FillQuoteTransformer, PayTakerTransformer, or other registered transformers
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker could redirect approved tokens via transformation pipeline or extract value through complex multi-step operations

economic_questions: Which 0x transformers are registered at the exchange proxy?
Can PayTakerTransformer redirect output to an arbitrary address?
Can FillQuoteTransformer consume residual approvals for other tokens?
validation_requirements: Enumerate registered transformers on mainnet 0x proxy
Test crafted transformations array against live 0x proxy
Verify if transformations can redirect output away from msg.sender

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to all residual approved tokens + redirect of swap output

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Other token depositors whose tokens have residual approvals
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to full residual approved amounts
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All tokens with residual approvals to 0x proxy

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Catalog all registered 0x transformers and their capabilities
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test transformation array manipulation against real proxy

threats.title: Residual Token Approval to Exchange Proxy Never Cleared After Swap
threats.hypothesis_description: _fillQuote approves exchangeProxy for _amount of _sellToken but never resets the approval to 0 after the swap completes. If the 0x swap results in a partial fill or if the approval isn't fully consumed, the residual approval persists. Any subsequent claim (by any user) that calls the exchange proxy could consume this residual approval, especially if combined with the ABI offset or TransformERC20 transformation bypasses.
threats.investigation_rationale: The approval is a simple approve() (not safeApprove, not reset-after-use). The 0x proxy retains any unconsumed allowance. Combined with the calldata validation bypasses (ECON-H002/H003), an attacker could craft calldata that consumes another token's residual approval through the exchange proxy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)) with no subsequent approve(exchangeProxy, 0) after swap execution

preconditions: Previous _fillQuote call resulted in partial fill (less than _amount consumed)
Exchange proxy retains residual allowance for the token
Attacker can craft calldata to consume the residual via bypass techniques

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Residual allowance persists between claims, consumable by any subsequent 0x interaction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals residual approved tokens by routing through manipulated calldata

economic_questions: Do standard 0x swaps always consume exact approved amounts?
Can TransformERC20 transformations result in partial fills?
What token allowances persist on mainnet 0x proxy from this contract?
validation_requirements: Test partial fill scenarios with 0x swap functions
Verify allowance state after various swap types
Combine with ABI bypass to test cross-token approval consumption

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full residual approved amounts per token

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After a partial-fill claim creates residual approval

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users whose tokens have residual approvals
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all residual approvals across token types

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check IERC20.allowance(address(this), exchangeProxy) for each token
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify 0x consumption patterns for various swap types

threats.title: Zero-Percentage LRT Claim Enables Repeatable ETH Drain Without Cost
threats.hypothesis_description: The _percentage parameter in _claim for non-ETH tokens is not validated to be in [1,100]. With small userStake values, userClaim = userStake * _percentage / 100 rounds to 0 due to integer division. The swap executes for 0 tokens (potentially succeeding as no-op), balance remains unchanged (userStake - 0 = userStake), but claimedAmount = address(this).balance captures all residual ETH. This can be repeated infinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This amplifies ECON-H001 into a repeatable zero-cost exploit. The attacker needs only 1 wei of LRT balance (cheaply obtained via lockFor) and can call claim repeatedly whenever ETH accumulates in the contract. The balance is never reduced since userStake - 0 = userStake.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userClaim = userStake * _percentage / 100 rounds to 0 for small stakes; balances[msg.sender][_token] = userStake - 0 = userStake (unchanged); claimedAmount = address(this).balance

preconditions: Attacker has small LRT balance (e.g., 1 wei via direct deposit)
startClaimDate has passed
Residual ETH exists in contract
0x proxy accepts 0-amount swap (or attacker crafts calldata that succeeds with 0 tokens)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: userStake=1, _percentage=99 → userClaim = 1*99/100 = 0; balance unchanged; claimedAmount = full ETH balance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Repeatable: call claim → drain ETH → balance unchanged → wait for more ETH → repeat
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Infinite repetition capturing any ETH that enters contract post-conversion

economic_questions: Does the 0x proxy accept and succeed on 0-amount swaps?
Can calldata be crafted to trivially succeed with 0 tokens?
What is the realistic flow of ETH into the contract post-conversion?
validation_requirements: Test 0-amount swap execution on 0x proxy (both UniswapV3 and TransformERC20)
Verify balance persistence after 0-amount claim
Confirm lpETH.deposit{value: N} works for any N > 0

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 1 wei of LRT token + gas for each drain tx
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near-infinite ROI per drain (gas cost only)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Anyone sending ETH to contract, protocol itself
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All future ETH that enters contract

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test _percentage values that cause rounding to 0

threats.title: Emergency Mode Cannot Rescue ETH Depositors After Conversion
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function checks 'if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead()' for ETH OUTSIDE the emergency mode conditional block. After convertAllETH sets startClaimDate, ETH depositors cannot withdraw even with emergencyMode enabled. They can only claim via lpETH.safeTransfer. If lpETH is broken/malicious, ETH is permanently locked. recoverERC20 also explicitly blocks lpETH recovery.
threats.investigation_rationale: The emergencyMode bypass only covers the time-window checks (loopActivation/startClaimDate), but the inner ETH-specific UseClaimInstead check runs regardless. This creates a state where no recovery path exists for ETH depositors if the lpETH contract fails after conversion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() has outer block 'if (!emergencyMode) { time checks }' but inner 'if (_token == ETH) { if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead(); }' is NOT gated by emergencyMode

preconditions: convertAllETH has been called (startClaimDate is set)
lpETH contract is non-functional, broken, or malicious
emergencyMode is enabled by owner

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: UseClaimInstead check at line 275 executes regardless of emergencyMode
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Post-conversion state + broken lpETH = permanently locked funds
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All ETH deposits permanently locked if lpETH fails (DoS/fund loss)

economic_questions: What happens if lpETH.safeTransfer reverts for all users?
Is there any alternative recovery path for ETH depositors?
Could owner deploy a new lpETH that unblocks transfers?
validation_requirements: Trace withdraw(ETH) execution path with emergencyMode=true and startClaimDate in past
Verify recoverERC20 cannot recover lpETH
Test claim path with reverting lpETH contract

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total ETH supply at risk of permanent lock

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: totalSupply worth of ETH (all ETH deposits)

threats.title: totalLpETH Inflated via lpETH Donation Before convertAllETH
threats.hypothesis_description: convertAllETH sets totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(address(this)) which includes ANY pre-existing lpETH in the contract, not just the amount received from the deposit. An attacker can send lpETH tokens to the contract before convertAllETH is called. This inflates totalLpETH relative to totalSupply, causing ETH depositors to claim more lpETH than their proportional share. The last claimer(s) will fail due to insufficient lpETH balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code uses balanceOf (absolute) rather than tracking the deposit return value (delta). If lpETH is obtainable before convertAllETH (e.g., from another source or earlier protocol deployment), an attacker who is also an ETH depositor profits from the inflated ratio.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(address(this)) instead of delta between pre/post deposit balance

preconditions: lpETH contract is deployed and accessible before convertAllETH
Attacker can obtain lpETH tokens to donate
Attacker is also an ETH depositor to benefit from inflated ratio

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(this) includes donated lpETH; claimedAmount = userStake * totalLpETH / totalSupply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Donate D lpETH → totalLpETH inflated by D → attacker's claim inflated by (userStake/totalSupply)*D
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker with 50% of totalSupply donating D lpETH captures D/2 extra lpETH; net cost D/2

economic_questions: Can lpETH be obtained independently before convertAllETH?
What is the attacker's net profit given donation cost?
Does the last claimer face insolvency from inflated claims?
validation_requirements: Verify lpETH is transferable before convertAllETH
Calculate profit for various stake ratios and donation sizes
Test last-claimer failure scenario

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: D lpETH for donation + ETH stake
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: (stake/totalSupply) * D extra lpETH minus D cost
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Only profitable if attacker has >50% of totalSupply

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Could lose partial or full claim amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model claim distribution with donated lpETH

threats.title: TransformERC20 Recipient Not Validated Enables Output Redirection
threats.hypothesis_description: In _validateData, the recipient check at the end validates that recipient is address(this) or address(0). For TransformERC20, _decodeTransformERC20Data never assigns recipient, so it defaults to address(0) and always passes. The actual swap output destination is determined by the unvalidated transformations array, not by a top-level recipient parameter. This means TransformERC20 output could be directed to any address via transformation steps.
threats.investigation_rationale: UniswapV3 explicitly validates recipient from decoded calldata. TransformERC20 has no recipient in its ABI signature that maps to the contract's recipient variable. The check passes vacuously for TransformERC20. Combined with unvalidated transformations (ECON-H003), swap output could be redirected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: TransformERC20 branch does not set 'recipient' variable; default address(0) always passes the recipient != address(this) && recipient != address(0) check

preconditions: Attacker uses TransformERC20 exchange path
0x registered transformers allow output redirection (e.g., PayTakerTransformer)
startClaimDate has passed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: recipient defaults to address(0) for TransformERC20, vacuously passing validation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transformations array could include PayTakerTransformer or similar to redirect output
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker redirects swap ETH output to own address; contract gets 0 ETH but claimedAmount could capture residual

economic_questions: Can PayTakerTransformer redirect output to an arbitrary address?
What is the actual output delivery mechanism of 0x TransformERC20?
Can an attacker receive swap output directly while contract receives nothing?
validation_requirements: Test TransformERC20 with output-redirecting transformations
Verify 0x default output delivery (msg.sender or taker)
Check if contract's claimedAmount = 0 when output redirected

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Swap output value + any residual ETH in contract
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Full swap value if successfully redirected

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Standard DEX liquidity for LRT→ETH swap

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol (lpETH receives 0 ETH deposit, attacker gets free lpETH from residual)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Attacker extracts full swap value outside protocol
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All LRT claim value that passes through TransformERC20

threats.title: Selfdestruct ETH Injection Inflates Conversion Ratio
threats.hypothesis_description: convertAllETH deposits address(this).balance (total contract ETH including unsolicited funds) to lpETH. An attacker can force-send ETH via selfdestruct before conversion, inflating totalBalance and thus totalLpETH. With totalLpETH > totalSupply, each ETH depositor's claim exceeds their actual stake. An attacker who is an ETH depositor profits proportionally from the inflation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The convertAllETH function trusts address(this).balance as the accurate amount of user-deposited ETH. The receive() function and selfdestruct allow injection of untracked ETH, creating a mismatch between totalSupply (tracked deposits) and actual ETH balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 totalBalance = address(this).balance; lpETH.deposit{value: totalBalance}; totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(this) — totalBalance includes unsolicited ETH

preconditions: Attacker has ETH to inject (via selfdestruct or receive)
Attacker is also an ETH depositor in the contract
convertAllETH has not yet been called

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalBalance = address(this).balance includes injected ETH; totalLpETH = totalBalance (assuming 1:1); but totalSupply only tracks real deposits
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Inject E extra ETH → totalLpETH = totalSupply + E → each user claims (stake * (totalSupply + E) / totalSupply) lpETH
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker with stake S injects E: gains S*E/totalSupply extra lpETH; costs E ETH; net profitable only if attacker has >50% stake

economic_questions: Is selfdestruct still viable on target chain (post-Dencun)?
What is the minimum profitable stake ratio for the attacker?
Does the last claimer face insolvency from over-claiming?
validation_requirements: Test selfdestruct ETH injection before convertAllETH
Calculate profit/loss for various stake ratios
Verify total claims vs totalLpETH solvency

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: E ETH for injection + existing stake
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: (stake/totalSupply) * E extra lpETH at cost of E ETH

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Last claimers may be unable to claim full amount

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Last ETH depositors to claim (insolvency risk)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: address(this).balance vs totalSupply at conversion time
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model claim distribution with injected ETH

threats.title: Owner Sets Malicious lpETH/Vault to Steal All Deposited ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: setLoopAddresses allows the owner to set arbitrary lpETH and lpETHVault addresses with no validation. A malicious or compromised owner can set lpETH to a contract that accepts ETH deposits but returns nothing or traps funds. After the 7-day TIMELOCK, convertAllETH sends ALL contract ETH to the malicious lpETH. Users who don't withdraw during the grace period lose all funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Single-owner model with no multisig, no governance vote, no address validation. The 7-day grace period is the only protection, but users must actively monitor. The addresses are permanent once set (loopActivation prevents re-calling setLoopAddresses).
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script PrelaunchPoints.s.sol shows owner is set from PRIVATE_KEY with no multisig wrapper

proof_of_signal.pattern: lpETH = ILpETH(_loopAddress); lpETHVault = ILpETHVault(_vaultAddress); no address validation, no multisig, permanent once set

preconditions: Owner key is compromised or owner is malicious
setLoopAddresses has not been called yet (before 120-day deadline)
Users don't withdraw during 7-day grace period

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: convertAllETH sends 100% of address(this).balance to owner-specified lpETH contract
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious lpETH contract accepts ETH but doesn't return lpETH tokens, or returns worthless tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals entire totalSupply worth of ETH

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk (totalSupply at deployment)?
How effective is the 7-day grace period as a protection mechanism?
Can the owner change addresses to malicious ones after initially setting legitimate ones?
validation_requirements: Confirm setLoopAddresses can only be called once
Verify 7-day TIMELOCK enforcement
Check if there's any way to reset addresses after initial setting

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant ETH deposits, before user awareness

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All ETH depositors who don't withdraw during grace period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify single-call restriction and TIMELOCK enforcement
